en.extract:
The pictorella mannikin, pictorella munia, or pictorella finch (Heteromunia pectoralis) is small brown and grey finch with a grey bill and distinctive scaly white breast plate which is endemic to northern Australia. It is a seed-eater found in pairs and small flocks in dry savannah and subtropical or tropical dry lowland grassland.


Taxonomy
The pictorella mannikin was formally described in 1841 by English ornithologist John Gould based on a specimen that had been collected in northwestern Australia by John E. Dring who had been a crew member on the third voyage of HMS Beagle. Gould coined the binomial name Amadina pectoralis. When in 1842 Gould described and illustrated the finch in the third volume of his book The Birds of Australia he instead placed the species in the genus Donacola. The pictorella mannikin is now the only species placed in the genus Heteromunia that was introduced by Gregory Mathews in 1913. The genus name combines the Ancient Greek ἑτερος/heteros meaning "different" or "another" with the genus name Munia that had been introduced in 1836 by Brian Houghton Hodgson. The specific epithet pectoralis is Latin meaning "of the breast" or "pectoral". The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.
The common name used in Australia is pictorella mannikin. Gould used the name white-breasted finch in his The Birds of Australia, and it was the common name until 1926 when the Royal Australasian Ornithologists Union (RAOU) Official checklist declared the common name to be "pectorella finch" with no explanation.
The name mannikin is from the Dutch manneken, a diminutive of man. There is no clarity as to why this has been applied to birds in general and particularly to seed eating finches. In 1978 the RAOU checklist committee recommended using "mannikin" for the Australian Lonchura species.


Description
The pictorella mannikin is a grey-billed, buff-brown and grey finch with a distinctive white scaly breast and black face disc. Small white tips on its wing coverts "impart a jewel-like appearance". The bird has a maximum size of 120mm, with a wingspan of 56-63mm, a bill of 12-14mm and weighs between 13 and 15 grams.


Behaviour and ecology
Pictorella mannikins forage on the ground in small flocks or pairs on seeding native grasses and sometimes take small invertebrates. Flocks aggregate around inland water sources particularly at the end of the dry season but disperse away from permanent water when rain falls. In northern Australia they often forage on recently burnt ground, open grassy woodland and fringes of wetland.
They are relatively short tailed with an upright stance. Pictorella mannikin pairs mate for life and lay 4 to 6 white or bluish white eggs in a dome-shaped grass nest in long grass or low bush.
In 2016 the pictorella mannikin's conservation status was listed Least Concern (LC) on the IUCN Red List although the population is marked as decreasing. In 2007, the International Union for Conservation of Nature downgraded its assessment of the species from Near Threatened (NT) to Least Concern (LC) after large flocks of this species were found at several locations.  In the Northern Territory and Western Australia the bird's conservation status is listed as Near Threatened (NT) while in Queensland the conservation status listing is Least Concern (LC).

The '''pictorella mannikin''', '''pictorella munia''', or '''pictorella finch''' ('''''Heteromunia pectoralis''''') is small brown and grey [[Estrildidae|finch]] with a grey [[Beak|bill]] and distinctive scaly white breast plate which is [[Endemism|endemic]] to northern [[Australia]]. It is a [[Seed eater|seed-eater]] found in pairs and small flocks in dry [[savannah]] and subtropical or tropical dry lowland [[grassland]].

==Taxonomy==
The pictorella mannikin was [[species description|formally described]] in 1841 by English ornithologist [[John Gould]] based on a specimen that had been collected in northwestern Australia by John E. Dring who had been a crew member on the [[HMS_Beagle#Third_voyage_(1837–1843)|third voyage of HMS Beagle]]. Gould coined the [[binomial name]] ''Amadina pectoralis''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | date=1840 | publication-date=July 1841 | title=''Amadina pectoralis'' | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume=8 | issue=93 | page=127 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30571566 }} For the publication date see: {{ cite journal | last=Sclater | first=P.L. | author-link=Philip Sclater | date=1893 | title=List of the dates of delivery of the sheets of the 'Proceedings' of the Zoological Society of London, from the commencement in 1830 to 1859 inclusive | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | pages=436–440 | url=http://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35992700 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1968 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=14 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=387 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14481588 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | last=Stokes | first=John Lort | author-link=John Lort Stokes | date=1846 | title=Discoveries in Australia; with an account of the coasts and rivers explored and surveyed during the voyage of H.M.S. Beagle, in the years 1837-38-39-40-41-42-43. | volume=1 | location=London | publisher=T. and W. Boone | pages=[https://archive.org/details/b29350359_0001/page/25/mode/1up 25],[https://archive.org/details/b29350359_0001/page/138/mode/1up 138], [https://archive.org/details/b29350359_0001/page/480/mode/1up 480] }}</ref> When in 1842 Gould described and illustrated the finch in the third volume of his book ''[[The Birds of Australia (Gould)|The Birds of Australia]]'' he instead placed the species in the genus ''Donacola''.<ref name=boa>{{ cite book | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1848 | publication-date=1842 | title=The Birds of Australia | publisher=self | location=London | volume=3 | at=Part 7, Plate 95 and text | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48286113 }} The 7 volumes were published in 36 parts. For the date see: {{ cite book | last=Waterhouse | first=Frederick Herschel | date=1885 | title=The Dates of Publication of Some of the Zoological Works of the Late John Gould, F.R.S | location=London | publisher=R.H. Porter | pages=28–44 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12782739 }}</ref> The pictorella mannikin is now the only species placed in the genus ''Heteromunia'' that was introduced by [[Gregory Mathews]] in 1913.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Waxbills, parrotfinches, munias, whydahs, Olive Warbler, accentors, pipits | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/waxbills/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=9 August 2025 }}</ref> The genus name combines the [[Ancient Greek]] ἑτερος/''heteros'' meaning "different" or "another" with the genus name ''Munia'' that had been introduced in 1836 by [[Brian Houghton Hodgson]].<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=Heteromunia | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=Heteromunia | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=9 August 2025 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''pectoralis'' is [[Latin]] meaning "of the breast" or "pectoral".<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Gould|first=J|date=1841|title=Untitled|journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London|volume=1841|issue=3|pages=126–127: Date published Jul 1841}}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>

The common name used in Australia is pictorella mannikin.<ref name="Names">{{Cite book|author-link=Ian Fraser (naturalist)|last1=Fraser|first1=Ian|title=Australian Bird Names: Origins and Meanings|last2=Gray|first2=Jeannie|publisher=CSIRO Publishing|year=2019|isbn=978-1-4863-1163-7|location=Clayton South, Vic|pages=281–288}}</ref><ref name=Menkhorst/> Gould used the name white-breasted finch in his ''The Birds of Australia'',<ref name=boa/> and it was the common name until 1926 when the [[Royal Australasian Ornithologists Union]] (RAOU) Official checklist declared the common name to be "pectorella finch" with no explanation.<ref name="Names"/><ref>{{Cite book|last=Wolstenholme |first=H. |year=1926 |title=The Official Checklist of the Birds of Australia | edition=2nd | location=Wahroonga, Sydney | publisher=Royal Australasian Ornithologists Union | page=107 | url=https://archive.org/details/officialcheckli00wols/page/107/mode/1up }}</ref>

The name mannikin is from the [[Dutch language|Dutch]] ''manneken'', a diminutive of man. There is no clarity as to why this has been applied to birds in general and particularly to seed eating finches.<ref name="Names"/> In 1978 the RAOU checklist committee recommended using "mannikin" for the Australian ''[[Lonchura]]'' species.<ref>{{Cite journal|date=1978|title=Recommended English Names for Australian Birds|journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology|volume=77:sup1|pages=245–307|doi=10.1071/MU9770245s |bibcode=1978EmuAO..77S.245. }}</ref><ref name="Names"/>

== Description ==
The pictorella mannikin is a grey-billed, buff-brown and grey finch with a distinctive white scaly breast and black face disc.<ref name=Slater>{{Cite book|author-link=Peter Slater (ornithologist)|last1=Slater|first1=Peter|title=[[The Slater Field Guide to Australian Birds]]|last2=Slater|first2=Pat|last3=Slater|first3=Raoul|publisher=Reed New Holland|year=2009|isbn=978-1-877069-63-5|edition=2nd|location=London, Sydney, Auckland|pages=394–395}}</ref><ref name=Menkhorst>{{Cite book|author-link=Peter Menkhorst|last1=Menkhorst|first1=Peter|title=[[The Australian Bird Guide]]|last2=Rogers|first2=Danny|last3=Clarke|first3=Rohan|last4=Davies|first4=Jeff|last5=Marsack|first5=Peter|last6=Franklin|first6=Kim|publisher=CSIRO Publishing|year=2017|isbn=978-0-643-09754-4|location=Clayton, Vic|pages=518–519}}</ref> Small white tips on its [[wing]] [[Covert feather|coverts]] "impart a jewel-like appearance".<ref name=Menkhorst/> The bird has a maximum size of 120mm,<ref name=Atlas>{{Cite web|title=Species: Heteromunia pectoralis (Pictorella Mannikin)|url=https://bie.ala.org.au/species/urn:lsid:biodiversity.org.au:afd.taxon:f78e5c3f-e48d-471d-8477-de2bdaae79d7|last=Australia|first=Atlas of Living|website=bie.ala.org.au|language=en-AU|access-date=2020-05-03}}</ref> with a [[wingspan]] of 56-63mm, a bill of 12-14mm and weighs between 13 and 15 grams.<ref name=Menkhorst/>

== Behaviour and ecology ==
Pictorella mannikins forage on the ground in small [[Flock (birds)|flocks]] or pairs on seeding native grasses<ref name=Menkhorst/><ref name=Slater/> and sometimes take small [[invertebrate]]s.<ref name=Atlas/> Flocks aggregate around inland water sources particularly at the end of the dry season<ref name=Menkhorst/><ref name=Slater/> but disperse away from permanent water when rain falls.<ref name=Atlas/> In northern Australia they often forage on recently burnt ground, open grassy woodland and fringes of wetland.<ref name=Menkhorst/>

They are relatively short tailed with an upright stance.<ref name=Menkhorst/> Pictorella mannikin pairs mate for life and lay 4 to 6 white or bluish white eggs in a dome-shaped grass nest in long grass or low bush.<ref name=Slater/><ref name=Atlas/>

In 2016 the pictorella mannikin's [[conservation status]] was listed Least Concern (LC) on the [[IUCN Red List]] although the population is marked as decreasing. In 2007, the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] downgraded its assessment of the species from [[near threatened|Near Threatened]] (NT) to [[least concern|Least Concern]] (LC) after large flocks of this species were found at several locations.<ref name="iucn" />  In the Northern Territory and Western Australia the bird's conservation status is listed as Near Threatened (NT) while in Queensland the conservation status listing is Least Concern (LC).<ref name=Atlas/>

zh.extract:
斑胸文鸟（学名：Heteromunia pectoralis），是梅花雀科斑胸文鸟属的一种，是游猎迁徙的候鸟，为澳大利亚的特有种。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
斑胸文鸟的平均体重约为15.2克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的旱林、干燥的稀树草原、耕地和亚热带或热带的（低地）干草原。


参考文献
